_meta.title: Modern Patterns — Functional Core / Imperative Shell

questions.question: You need to unit test a discount calculation that currently reads customer tier from the database inside the same function. What is the most testable refactor?
options: Inject a mock database interface into the function
Extract the discount calculation into a pure function that takes the tier as an argument
Use an in-memory database for tests
Make the database read lazy so it only runs in production
questions.explanation: A pure function that takes tier as an argument needs no mocks, no database, and no infrastructure. This is the FC/IS pattern: the core is pure, the shell provides the data.

questions.question: What is the defining property of the 'functional core' in the Functional Core / Imperative Shell pattern?
options: It uses functional programming language features like monads
It is referentially transparent — same inputs always produce same outputs, no side effects
It runs on a functional programming language like Haskell or Erlang
It encapsulates all database access behind interfaces
questions.explanation: The functional core's defining property is referential transparency: no side effects, no dependencies on I/O. You can replace any call with its return value. The language doesn't matter — the pattern works in Rust, TypeScript, Python, or Java.

questions.question: How does Functional Core / Imperative Shell differ from Hexagonal Architecture?
options: They are the same pattern with different names
Hexagonal allows side effects through abstract ports; FC/IS forbids side effects in the core entirely
FC/IS uses interfaces while hexagonal uses pure functions
Hexagonal is for microservices; FC/IS is for monoliths
questions.explanation: In hexagonal architecture, the core may call abstract ports (e.g., userRepository.findById()) — these are still side effects, just behind interfaces, requiring mocks to test. FC/IS forbids side effects in the core: the shell calls the repository, passes data into the pure core, and the core returns a decision. No mocks needed.

questions.question: Your OrderService.process() method validates the order, calculates a discount, saves to the database, and sends an HTTP notification — all in one method. How do you apply FC/IS?
options: Add dependency injection for the database and HTTP client so you can mock them in tests
Extract validate and calculate into pure functions; the shell calls them, then handles the database write and HTTP call
Split the method into four smaller methods on the same class
Wrap the method in a transaction scope so the database write and HTTP call are atomic
questions.explanation: FC/IS says: extract all decisions (validate, calculate) into pure functions that return data. The shell (the original method, now thin) calls these pure functions, interprets their output, and handles the effects (DB write, HTTP call). The pure functions are trivially testable with plain assertions.

questions.question: What is a 'Decision type' in the FC/IS pattern?
options: A type that represents a database entity
A sum type (enum/union) returned by the functional core describing what the shell should do
A type that wraps side effects in a monad
An interface that the shell must implement
questions.explanation: The functional core returns a Decision — a sum type like Accept(PricedOrder) | Reject(ValidationError). The core doesn't execute anything; it describes what should happen. The shell interprets the decision and executes the effects.

questions.question: Why are FC/IS integration tests expected to be few and slow, while unit tests are many and fast?
options: Because integration tests are less important than unit tests
Because the shell has no business logic — it's just wiring — so you only need a few integration tests, while the core contains all logic and is tested with thousands of fast pure-function tests
Because you should mock everything in unit tests and only test the shell end-to-end
Because the functional core is small and doesn't need many tests
questions.explanation: The shell contains only wiring code (read input → call core → write output). There's almost no logic to test. The core contains all business logic and is pure, so you can run thousands of property-based and example-based tests in milliseconds. Integration tests for the shell are necessary but few.
